children.title: Performance Test Types
children.description: Confusing load, stress, spike, and soak tests leads to testing the wrong thing at the wrong time. Each type answers a different business question, and selecting the wrong one means you can ship a system that fails under real-world conditions you never simulated.

children.title: Response Time Percentiles
children.description: Average response times hide the worst user experiences; a system with a 200ms average but 10-second p99 is unacceptable to 1 in 100 users. Understanding p50, p90, and p99 is essential for setting meaningful SLAs and identifying tail-latency problems.

children.title: JMeter Architecture
children.description: Without understanding JMeter thread groups, samplers, and listeners, test plans become unreliable and hard to maintain. Misconfigured ramp-up times and missing cookie managers produce results that do not reflect real user behavior.

children.title: HTTP Protocol for Performance Testers
children.description: Performance issues often originate at the protocol level — keep-alive connections, HTTP/2 multiplexing, and compression settings all affect throughput. A tester unfamiliar with HTTP internals will miss tuning opportunities and misread network-level bottlenecks.

children.title: Installing and Navigating JMeter
children.description: JMeter's UI is dense and non-obvious; testers who skip the guided walkthrough often build flawed test plans with silent configuration errors. Knowing where listeners, config elements, and assertions live saves hours of debugging failed runs.

children.title: Key Metrics: Throughput, Latency, Error Rate
children.description: Collecting the wrong metrics leads to false confidence — a system can show low latency while silently returning errors on 20% of requests. Tracking throughput, latency, and error rate together gives a complete picture of system health under load.

children.title: Bottleneck Identification Concepts
children.description: Without a framework for bottleneck thinking, teams fix symptoms rather than root causes — adding hardware to a system that simply has an unindexed SQL query. Understanding CPU, I/O, and memory saturation as distinct failure modes guides targeted investigation.

children.title: Virtual Users and Concurrency Model
children.description: Misunderstanding virtual users versus real users causes wildly inaccurate load simulations; 100 VUs sending requests without think time is not the same as 100 real users. Applying realistic think times and pacing transforms a stress test into an accurate load model.

children.title: Thread Groups and Ramp-Up
children.description: A ramp-up that is too fast triggers a spike test by accident, producing unreliable results that do not represent steady-state performance. Proper ramp-up configuration ensures the system reaches target load gradually, mimicking real traffic growth.

children.title: CSV Data Set Config
children.description: Tests that reuse the same username and password hit a cached or pre-warmed code path rather than exercising the full system, producing deceptively fast results. Parameterizing with CSV data ensures each virtual user follows a unique, realistic path.

children.title: Response Assertions
children.description: Without assertions, a test that receives HTTP 500 errors for every request still appears to complete successfully, giving false confidence. Response assertions enforce correctness so that throughput figures only count valid responses.

children.title: Summary Report and Aggregate Listener
children.description: The View Results Tree listener is only safe for small debug runs; leaving it enabled under load causes JMeter itself to become a bottleneck that skews all measurements. Using the Aggregate Report or Summary Report listener keeps overhead minimal while preserving statistical accuracy.

children.title: HTTP Cookie and Header Managers
children.description: Missing a Cookie Manager means sessions are not maintained between requests, causing every sampler after login to receive a 401 and completely invalidating the test. Header Managers ensure content-type and authorization headers are sent correctly on every request.

children.title: Timers and Think Time
children.description: Removing think time between requests creates an unrealistic hammer-load that no real user ever generates, pushing servers into behaviors never seen in production. Gaussian or uniform random timers reproduce the natural pause between user actions and yield credible throughput figures.

children.title: Non-GUI Mode Execution
children.description: Running JMeter with the GUI during a load test consumes significant CPU and memory on the injector machine, degrading the load generator itself and producing inaccurate results. Non-GUI mode (CLI) is the only supported way to run real load tests.

children.title: Distributed Load Testing with JMeter
children.description: A single JMeter instance can only generate a finite number of virtual users before the machine saturates; distributed mode lets you scale across multiple injectors to simulate thousands of concurrent users. Without this knowledge, tests are capped well below production traffic levels.

children.title: k6 Script Structure and VU Model
children.description: k6 scripts are JavaScript modules with a default exported function that every virtual user executes in a loop; misunderstanding this lifecycle causes shared state bugs that corrupt results. Knowing how setup, teardown, and the default function interact is essential for correct test design.

children.title: Checks and Thresholds
children.description: Checks in k6 are non-blocking pass/fail assertions on individual responses, while thresholds are the SLA gates that fail the entire test run when breached. Conflating the two leads to tests that report failures but still exit with code 0, silently passing CI gates.

children.title: Ramping VUs with Stages
children.description: Stages define the shape of a load test — without them every test is a flat constant-load test that cannot simulate warm-up, peak, or cool-down phases. Incorrect stage configuration means you never validate the system's behavior during the critical ramp-up transition.

children.title: Custom Metrics
children.description: Built-in k6 metrics cover HTTP but not business-level events like checkout completion rate or search result relevance; custom metrics fill that gap. Without them, performance reports cannot correlate technical degradation with actual user-journey impact.

children.title: k6 Output to InfluxDB
children.description: The default k6 terminal summary disappears when the run ends; streaming output to InfluxDB persists all time-series data for post-run analysis and trend comparison. Without a persistent store, you cannot compare runs across days or detect gradual performance regressions.

children.title: Real-Time Grafana Dashboard for k6
children.description: Waiting until after a test finishes to analyze results means you cannot abort an out-of-control test before it causes an outage; a real-time Grafana dashboard enables live go/no-go decisions. The official k6 Grafana dashboard template provides pre-built panels for all key metrics.

children.title: k6 JavaScript API
children.description: k6 exposes a rich JavaScript API covering HTTP, WebSockets, gRPC, and browser automation; knowing the API surface prevents reinventing functionality that is already built in. Using the wrong module (e.g., Node.js fetch instead of k6 http) silently breaks the VU execution model.

children.title: k6 Extensions and xk6
children.description: When built-in protocols are insufficient, k6's extension system (xk6) lets you compile custom Go-based extensions into the k6 binary without forking the project. Ignoring extensions means developers write unsupported workarounds that break between k6 versions.

children.title: Throughput and Requests Per Second
children.description: Throughput defines the volume of work a system can sustain; without a baseline RPS target, you cannot determine whether a given load level is within normal operating range or represents stress. SLAs that omit throughput minimums leave teams unable to tell if a slow system is degraded or simply under-loaded.

children.title: Error Rate and Apdex Score
children.description: An error rate above 1% under load often signals resource exhaustion or connection pool limits that latency metrics alone do not reveal. Apdex translates raw latency into a user satisfaction index, making performance results accessible to non-technical stakeholders.

children.title: Establishing a Performance Baseline
children.description: Without a baseline, every performance regression is invisible because there is nothing to compare against; the first load test result is worthless until a second run validates it. Baselines must be established under controlled conditions before any optimization work begins.

resources.label: How to Create a Performance Baseline

children.title: SLA vs SLO vs SLI
children.description: Confusing SLAs (contractual commitments), SLOs (internal targets), and SLIs (raw measurements) causes teams to chase the wrong number or fail audits. Performance test thresholds should be set against SLOs, which provide headroom before the SLA boundary is breached.

resources.label: SLI SLO SLA - Google SRE Book

children.title: Prometheus Metrics and Exporters
children.description: Application metrics exposed via Prometheus exporters reveal what the system is doing internally while JMeter or k6 applies load; without them, you only see symptoms on the client side and not the cause on the server side. Node exporter, JVM exporter, and custom business metrics form a complete observability stack.

children.title: Grafana Annotations for Test Events
children.description: Annotations mark the exact moment a load test started and stopped on every Grafana panel, making before/after comparisons immediate and unambiguous. Without them, correlating a latency spike with the corresponding test run requires manual timestamp math across multiple tools.

resources.label: Using Grafana Annotations in Load Tests

children.title: JMeter HTML Dashboard Report
children.description: JMeter's built-in HTML dashboard generates rich statistical charts from raw CSV results without requiring a separate database, making it the fastest way to share results with stakeholders. Missing this feature leads teams to screenshot Summary Report rows and paste them into slide decks, losing statistical context.

children.title: Performance KPI Dashboard Design
children.description: A dashboard cluttered with 40 panels is harder to act on than one with 6 well-chosen KPIs; performance dashboards must be designed for quick go/no-go decisions, not comprehensive data dumping. The right structure separates user-facing metrics from infrastructure metrics across distinct rows.

children.title: k6 Scenarios and Executors
children.description: Scenarios let you model multiple independent user workflows within a single test run, each with its own executor, VU count, and thresholds. Without scenarios, you are forced to run separate tests for each workflow, making it impossible to observe how concurrent workloads interact.

children.title: k6 Modules and Code Organization
children.description: Monolithic k6 scripts become unmaintainable above a few hundred lines; extracting shared utilities, page helpers, and fixture data into ES modules enables reuse and clarity. k6's module system differs from Node.js, and importing Node.js-only packages silently fails at runtime.

children.title: InfluxDB Data Model
children.description: InfluxDB organizes performance data into measurements, tags, and fields; writing queries without understanding this model returns misleading aggregations. Correct tag selection determines whether you can filter by test run, VU count, or scenario without full table scans.

children.title: Grafana Alerting on Performance Regressions
children.description: Manual dashboard watching during a 2-hour soak test is unreliable; Grafana alerts fire automatically when metrics cross SLO thresholds, freeing engineers for other work. Poorly configured alert evaluation intervals cause either missed incidents or alert storms.

children.title: Correlating Client and Server Metrics
children.description: Client-side latency and server-side CPU can diverge significantly when network delays or load balancers are involved; overlaying both on the same Grafana timeline reveals where time is actually being spent. Treating client and server metrics in isolation leads to misattributed root causes.

resources.label: Correlating Load Test and App Metrics

children.title: Exporting and Sharing Dashboards
children.description: Dashboards that exist only in one Grafana instance cannot be version-controlled or shared with distributed teams; exporting as JSON and committing to a repository makes dashboards reproducible. Grafana's provisioning system automates dashboard deployment across environments.

children.title: Abort Criteria During Live Tests
children.description: Running a load test to completion when error rates have already reached 50% wastes time and risks destabilizing production-adjacent environments. Defining explicit abort thresholds in advance turns a potentially destructive test into a controlled safety exercise.

children.title: Prometheus Remote Write for k6
children.description: k6 can stream metrics directly to a Prometheus-compatible remote write endpoint, removing the InfluxDB dependency and fitting into existing monitoring stacks. Teams that skip this integration often maintain two separate observability stacks, one for load testing and one for production.

children.title: CPU and Memory Profiling with Java VisualVM
children.description: Load tests that show slow response times without profiling JVM internals leave you guessing between CPU-bound computation, GC pauses, and lock contention. VisualVM connects live to any JVM and reveals which methods consume the most CPU and how memory allocations behave under concurrent load.

children.title: Thread Dump Analysis
children.description: When throughput drops unexpectedly without rising CPU, the culprit is usually lock contention visible only in thread dumps. A single thread dump snapshot is rarely enough; capturing three dumps 10 seconds apart reveals whether threads are stuck or simply waiting on I/O.

resources.label: How to Take a Thread Dump

children.title: Slow Query Identification with EXPLAIN
children.description: Database queries are the most common source of performance bottlenecks in web applications, yet they are invisible to load testing tools without deliberate instrumentation. Running EXPLAIN ANALYZE on queries flagged by the slow query log reveals missing indexes and full table scans in seconds.

children.title: Connection Pool Saturation
children.description: Application servers that exhaust their database connection pool under load return errors even though the database itself has spare capacity; this manifests as a sharp error rate spike that looks like an application crash. Monitoring pool metrics alongside load metrics is essential for diagnosing this pattern.

children.title: USE Method: Utilization, Saturation, Errors
children.description: Brendan Gregg's USE method provides a systematic checklist for every system resource, ensuring no bottleneck is overlooked during root cause analysis. Without a structured methodology, engineers jump between random metrics and miss the resource that is actually saturated.

children.title: Flame Graph Interpretation
children.description: Flame graphs collapse stack traces from thousands of profiler samples into a single visualization, making it possible to identify the call path consuming the most CPU at a glance. The width of a frame is proportional to its CPU time, not call depth, which is counterintuitive to engineers reading them for the first time.

children.title: Garbage Collection Analysis
children.description: JVM GC pauses cause stop-the-world latency spikes that appear as periodic high p99 values in load test results; without GC logs, these spikes look like application bugs. Enabling GC logging and analyzing it with GCViewer or GCEasy reveals pause frequency, duration, and heap pressure.

children.title: Heap Dump Analysis
children.description: Memory leaks in long-running services only become visible during soak tests; a heap dump captured at the peak reveals which objects are retained and which classes are responsible. Without heap dump analysis skills, memory leaks are addressed by increasing heap size rather than fixing the root cause.

children.title: Finding the Breaking Point
children.description: Stress testing increments load beyond expected peak until the system degrades or fails, revealing the true capacity ceiling and failure mode. Without this knowledge, capacity planning relies on guesswork and systems fail in production at loads that were never tested.

children.title: System Recovery Validation
children.description: A system that degrades gracefully under stress but never recovers is as dangerous as one that crashes; validating auto-recovery after stress test completion is a distinct and mandatory test assertion. Recovery time objectives (RTOs) should be defined before the stress test begins.

children.title: Spike Test Design
children.description: Spike tests simulate sudden traffic bursts — flash sales, viral events, or DDoS attacks — that neither load nor stress tests model. A system that handles 10x normal traffic gracefully when gradually ramped may fail completely when the same load arrives within 30 seconds.

children.title: Defining Pass/Fail Criteria per Test Type
children.description: Applying load test pass/fail criteria to a stress test will always produce a failure, since stress tests are designed to exceed normal operating limits. Each test type needs its own thresholds: error rate tolerance for spike tests is higher than for soak tests.

children.title: Soak Test Duration and Monitoring
children.description: Memory leaks and connection pool exhaustion are invisible in short tests because they accumulate slowly; soak tests run at moderate load for 2-24 hours specifically to surface time-dependent degradation. Monitoring memory and error rate trends rather than just instantaneous values is the only way to detect a leak.

resources.label: Memory Leak Detection via Soak Tests

children.title: Capacity Planning from Test Data
children.description: Stress test results translate directly into horizontal scaling rules: knowing the RPS at which error rate exceeds 1% tells you exactly when to add nodes. Without this data, autoscaling thresholds are set by intuition rather than evidence, leading to both over-provisioning and unexpected capacity failures.

children.title: Breakpoint Testing
children.description: Breakpoint testing automates the process of incrementally increasing load until a target metric crosses a threshold, removing the need for manual binary-search style runs. This produces a precise capacity number rather than a range, improving infrastructure cost modeling accuracy.

children.title: Chaos Engineering Overlap with Performance
children.description: Chaos engineering injects failures during load tests to validate that performance SLOs hold even when individual components fail; this is the intersection where resilience and performance testing meet. Teams that treat the two disciplines as separate miss the most production-realistic scenarios.

children.title: k6 in GitHub Actions
children.description: Running performance tests in CI ensures that no code change degrades response times without immediate visibility; without CI integration, performance tests run sporadically and regressions accumulate undetected across many releases. GitHub Actions' matrix strategy enables parallel execution across multiple test scenarios.

children.title: Automated Threshold-Based Pass/Fail in CI
children.description: A performance test that always exits with code 0 cannot block a PR, making CI integration meaningless; k6 thresholds that exit with code 99 on breach provide the CI gate that enforces SLOs. Every CI performance job must define exit-code-sensitive pipeline conditions.

children.title: Containerized k6 Test Environments
children.description: Running k6 from a Docker container guarantees identical tool versions and network conditions across every CI runner, eliminating "works on my machine" inconsistencies. The official k6 Docker image requires no installation step and integrates cleanly into existing Docker-based pipelines.

children.title: Docker Compose for Full Stack Performance Testing
children.description: Testing a microservice in isolation ignores the performance impact of its downstream dependencies; Docker Compose brings up the entire stack in CI with a single command, enabling realistic integration-level performance tests. Service health checks in the Compose file prevent tests from starting before dependencies are ready.

children.title: Nightly Performance Regression Runs
children.description: Smoke-level performance tests on every PR are fast but shallow; nightly soak and load tests against a staging environment catch regressions that only appear under sustained traffic. Separating nightly jobs from PR jobs by test depth prevents CI from becoming too slow to use.

children.title: Alerting on Performance Regressions in CI
children.description: A failed CI performance job that sends no notification relies on engineers actively checking pipeline status; Slack or email alerts on threshold breaches ensure immediate response before the regression reaches production. Alert messages should include the specific threshold breached and a direct link to the Grafana dashboard.

children.title: Performance Trend Dashboards for CI
children.description: Point-in-time test results cannot answer whether this release is faster or slower than last week; trend dashboards plotting p95 latency and error rate per Git commit make regressions and improvements immediately visible to the team. k6 Cloud and self-hosted InfluxDB both support this pattern.

children.title: Flakiness Detection in Performance Tests
children.description: A performance test that sometimes passes and sometimes fails at the same load level is providing noise, not signal, and will eventually be ignored or disabled by the team. Statistical variance analysis over multiple runs distinguishes genuine regressions from natural measurement fluctuation.

children.title: API-Level vs UI-Level Performance Testing
children.description: UI-level performance tests are 5-10x slower to execute than API-level tests and introduce browser rendering variability that obscures server-side performance signals. Testing at the API layer first isolates backend performance from frontend concerns and produces faster, more actionable feedback.

children.title: Isolating Microservice Performance
children.description: Testing a microservice through an API gateway conflates the gateway's latency with the service's latency, making it impossible to identify which component is degraded. Direct service testing with controlled request rates isolates each service's true performance characteristics.

children.title: Distributed Tracing with Jaeger
children.description: In a microservices architecture, a slow end-to-end response time is caused by exactly one span in one service; distributed tracing finds that span in seconds while manual log correlation would take hours. Jaeger provides the UI to visualize span waterfalls and compare slow traces against fast baselines.

children.title: OpenTelemetry for Performance Context
children.description: OpenTelemetry provides a vendor-neutral standard for emitting traces, metrics, and logs from any language; instrumenting services with OTel means you can switch backends from Jaeger to Tempo without rewriting instrumentation. Without tracing context, performance problems in service mesh architectures are nearly impossible to diagnose.

children.title: Performance Testing in Docker Environments
children.description: Containerized services have different networking characteristics than bare-metal deployments, including overlay network latency and container resource limits that act as artificial ceilings. Performance test environments must match production container resource constraints to produce valid results.

children.title: Service Mesh Performance Considerations
children.description: Service meshes like Istio add a sidecar proxy to every pod, introducing measurable latency per hop that accumulates significantly in deep call chains. Performance testers who ignore the mesh underestimate end-to-end latency in production and miscalibrate SLO thresholds.

children.title: Consumer-Driven Contract Performance Checks
children.description: Consumer contracts typically define functional expectations but rarely include latency constraints; adding performance assertions to Pact contracts prevents providers from shipping functional changes that silently degrade response time. This bridges the gap between contract testing and performance testing disciplines.

children.title: Integration Performance Test Design
children.description: Integration performance tests target the seams between services rather than individual endpoints, revealing cascade effects where one slow downstream call doubles the latency of multiple upstream services. Identifying these amplification points is only possible with cross-service instrumentation.

children.title: Defining SLAs and SLOs for APIs
children.description: SLOs without SLAs are aspirational targets with no accountability; SLAs without SLOs have no early warning system before contractual breach. Establishing both in terms of p95 latency and error rate per endpoint creates a two-tier system that gives the team time to react before customers are impacted.

children.title: Performance Baseline Establishment
children.description: A performance report that says "p95 latency is 450ms" is only actionable when compared against a baseline; without the baseline, stakeholders cannot determine if 450ms represents improvement, regression, or expected behavior. Baseline runs must be conducted under identical environment conditions to be valid comparators.

children.title: Performance Trend Analysis Across Releases
children.description: Single-run comparisons between releases miss gradual regressions that accumulate across many small commits; trend charts over 10+ runs reveal slow drift before it becomes a production incident. Storing all test results in InfluxDB or a time-series store is a prerequisite for meaningful trend analysis.

children.title: Grafana Annotations for Test Events
children.description: Annotations create visual markers on Grafana timelines that align load test phases with metric changes, making it immediately clear that a latency increase started precisely when the load ramped to 500 VUs. Without annotations, correlating test events with metric changes requires manual timestamp arithmetic.

children.title: Executive Performance Test Reports
children.description: Technical engineers and business stakeholders need fundamentally different information from the same test run; an executive summary with pass/fail and business impact should accompany every detailed technical report. Reports that bury the conclusion in raw data tables go unread and unacted upon.

children.title: SLA Compliance Tables
children.description: SLA compliance tables compare each measured endpoint percentile against its defined threshold, producing a single at-a-glance pass/fail matrix that stakeholders can sign off on. Without this structure, performance reports invite endless discussion about which numbers matter instead of focusing on the binary compliance question.

children.title: Structured Recommendation Format
children.description: Performance recommendations without priority and effort estimates are wish lists, not action plans; ranking findings by performance impact versus implementation cost gives development teams a clear starting point. Each recommendation should include the specific metric improved, the expected gain, and the implementation risk.

children.title: Test Result Archiving Strategy
children.description: Without an archiving strategy, old test results are overwritten or lost, making baseline comparisons and trend analysis impossible months after the tests ran. Naming conventions that include the build number, environment, and timestamp in every result file enable instant retrieval for any historical comparison.

children.title: k6 CI/CD Integration Patterns
children.description: Performance tests in CI must be fast enough to not block developers while still providing meaningful signal; a tiered approach runs a 2-minute smoke test on every PR and a full load test nightly. Using k6's --stage flag and environment variables makes the same script executable at different load levels without code duplication.

children.title: Performance Gate in Pull Request Workflow
children.description: A performance gate that blocks PRs when p95 latency increases by more than 10% prevents incremental regressions from accumulating into a major incident. The gate must compare against a stable baseline, not just the previous run, to avoid accepting a regression that was introduced two PRs ago.

children.title: k6 Docker Image in CI
children.description: Using the official k6 Docker image in CI ensures reproducible test execution without managing tool installations on runners; a pinned image tag prevents silent k6 version upgrades from changing test behavior. The Docker approach also works identically on GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins.

children.title: InfluxDB and Grafana in Docker Compose for CI
children.description: Spinning up InfluxDB and Grafana via Docker Compose in the CI job creates a self-contained performance testing environment that requires no external services. This pattern enables full observability stack testing in ephemeral CI environments that are torn down after each run.

children.title: Performance Test Pyramid for CI
children.description: Just as functional testing has a pyramid of unit, integration, and E2E tests, performance testing needs a pyramid of smoke, load, and soak tests with different run frequencies. Without this structure, teams either run expensive tests too often and slow CI, or run cheap tests only and miss systemic regressions.

children.title: Alerting on Performance Regressions in CI
children.description: CI failure notifications sent only to the pipeline author miss regressions that affect the entire team's work; performance alerts should go to a shared channel with enough context to understand severity without opening the pipeline. Alert fatigue from overly sensitive thresholds causes engineers to start ignoring notifications entirely.

children.title: Grafana Snapshot Publishing from CI
children.description: A Grafana snapshot captures the dashboard state at a specific moment and generates a shareable URL without requiring the viewer to have Grafana access; posting this link as a PR comment gives reviewers instant access to performance results. Snapshots expire by default and must be configured with an appropriate retention period.

children.title: JUnit XML Performance Reports in CI
children.description: Most CI platforms natively render JUnit XML test reports as a summary table on the build page; generating JUnit XML from k6 or JMeter runs makes results browsable without navigating to Grafana. Failed assertions appear as individual test case failures, making it easy to spot which SLO was breached.
